h1: Space compliance: a complete guide to global regulation, standards, and licensing
meta_title: Space Compliance: Regulators, Standards & Licensing | Invariant
meta_description: A complete reference to space compliance: FAA Part 450, FCC Part 25, NOAA, IN-SPACe, ECSS, GEVS, ITU, qualification testing, and licensing timelines.
tldr: Space compliance is the integrated program of regulatory licensing, environmental qualification, frequency coordination, export control, and operational reporting that a commercial space operator executes from concept through end-of-life. In the United States it centers on the FAA Office of Commercial Space Transportation under 14 CFR Part 450, the FCC Space Bureau under 47 CFR Part 25, NOAA under 15 CFR Part 960, and the State Department and Commerce Department for export classification.
canonical_summary:
Space compliance is the body of regulatory licensing, environmental qualification, and operational obligations that a commercial space company must satisfy to legally design, launch, and operate spacecraft and launch vehicles. It spans three overlapping domains: (1) civil and national-security licensing by jurisdiction-specific regulators (FAA AST and FCC Space Bureau in the United States, NOAA's Office of Space Commerce for remote sensing, IN-SPACe in India, the UK's CAA, France's CNES, Japan's Cabinet Office, Australia's ASA), (2) technical qualification of flight hardware against environmental and interface standards (ECSS, NASA GSFC-STD-7000 / GEVS, MIL-STD-1540, MIL-STD-461 for EMI/EMC), and (3) international coordination obligations administered through the ITU Radio Regulations and the 1967 Outer Space Treaty regime.

In practice, space compliance is the gating activity between a working engineering design and a flight. A modern commercial mission typically requires parallel filings with three to six agencies, qualification testing across vibration, acoustic, shock, thermal-vacuum, EMI/EMC, and outgassing regimes, an orbital-debris mitigation showing under the FCC's five-year deorbit rule, ITU frequency coordination through the national administration, and export-control classification under ITAR or the EAR. Realistic licensing timelines run six to twenty-four months depending on jurisdiction and mission class, and missed compliance milestones are now the single most-cited cause of commercial launch slips. The field is shifting from prescriptive checklists toward performance-based regimes such as FAA Part 450 and risk-informed frameworks emerging at IN-SPACe and the UK CAA, which place more analytical burden on the operator.

sections.heading: What space compliance means
paragraphs: Space compliance is the discipline of meeting every regulatory, technical, and contractual obligation that stands between a spacecraft design and a flight. It is narrower than space law, which encompasses treaty interpretation and dispute resolution, and broader than launch licensing alone. A working definition: space compliance is the integrated programme of licensing, technical qualification, frequency coordination, export control, and ongoing operational reporting that an operator executes from concept through end-of-life disposal.

The term covers obligations imposed by three distinct sources. National regulators issue binding licences under domestic statutes such as the United States Commercial Space Launch Act, India's Indian Space Policy 2023, and the United Kingdom's Space Industry Act 2018. Standards bodies publish technical requirements that customers, primes, and insurers contractually incorporate; the European Cooperation for Space Standardization, NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, and the Department of Defense are the dominant sources. International instruments, principally the 1967 Outer Space Treaty and the ITU Radio Regulations, impose treaty-level obligations that flow through national administrations to operators.

What distinguishes space compliance from terrestrial regulated industries is the irreversibility of error. A non-compliant medical device can be recalled; a non-compliant spacecraft cannot. The discipline is therefore organised around pre-flight verification rather than post-market surveillance, and the documentary burden is consequently heavy: a single satellite licence application can run several thousand pages of safety analysis, link budgets, debris-mitigation showings, and operational procedures.
bullets: Licensing: launch, reentry, satellite radio, remote sensing, on-orbit operations, and disposal authorisations.
Technical qualification: environmental testing against ECSS, GEVS, MIL-STD-1540, and MIL-STD-461 baselines.
Spectrum coordination: ITU filings, FCC Part 25 authorisations, and national-administration assignments.
Export control: ITAR USML Category XV and EAR 9x515 classifications, deemed-export controls.
Treaty obligations: 1967 Outer Space Treaty, Liability Convention, Registration Convention, ITU Radio Regulations.

sections.heading: Who regulates space activities: a global map
paragraphs: No single body regulates space. Each launching state administers its own regime under the international treaty framework, and a commercial mission with international scope routinely touches three to six regulators. The map below traces the principal authorities by jurisdiction and notes the boundary lines that are most often misunderstood.

The United States operates the most fragmented regime. The Federal Aviation Administration's Office of Commercial Space Transportation, known as AST, licenses launch, reentry, and spaceports under 14 CFR. The Federal Communications Commission, through its Space Bureau established in 2023, licenses satellite radio transmissions and earth stations under 47 CFR Part 25 and enforces the orbital-debris rules. The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration's Office of Space Commerce licenses private remote sensing under 15 CFR Part 960. The Department of State administers the International Traffic in Arms Regulations for space-related defence articles, while the Department of Commerce's Bureau of Industry and Security handles dual-use export controls under the Export Administration Regulations.

India consolidated authority in October 2021 by establishing IN-SPACe, the Indian National Space Promotion and Authorisation Centre, as the single-window regulator for all non-governmental space activity. IN-SPACe operates under the Department of Space and issues authorisations across six activity categories: satellite establishment and operation, launch-vehicle activities, ground systems, dissemination of earth-observation data, suborbital activities, and on-orbit asset transfers. The Directorate General of Civil Aviation's space remit is narrowly limited to airspace clearance for launches and is not the primary space-licensing authority, a distinction frequently confused in industry coverage.

In the United Kingdom a two-track regime operates: the Outer Space Act 1986 governs UK-person activities conducted overseas, while the Space Industry Act 2018 covers UK-domestic launch and orbital operations. Both are administered by the Civil Aviation Authority's Space Regulation team, with the UK Space Agency holding policy and promotional responsibilities. The European Space Agency is not a licensing regulator: it is a research and procurement agency whose contracts mandate ECSS standards tailoring. National licensing within Europe is a member-state matter, most prominently CNES in France under the 2008 Loi sur les Opérations Spatiales, BMWK and DLR in Germany, and the Luxembourg Space Agency. Japan operates through the Cabinet Office's National Space Policy Secretariat under the 2016 Space Activities Act; JAXA is the national space agency, not the regulator. Australia regulates through the Australian Space Agency under the Space (Launches and Returns) Act 2018.

The International Telecommunication Union sits above the national regulators for spectrum and orbit. The ITU Radiocommunication Sector maintains the Radio Regulations treaty, coordinates geostationary and non-geostationary orbital slots, and records frequency assignments in the Master International Frequency Register. Operators do not file directly with the ITU; they file through their national administration, which forwards the submission.
bullets: United States: FAA AST (launch and reentry), FCC Space Bureau (radio), NOAA Office of Space Commerce (remote sensing), State Department (ITAR), Commerce BIS (EAR).
India: IN-SPACe as single-window authoriser; ISRO as national agency; NSIL as commercial arm.
United Kingdom: Civil Aviation Authority administers OSA 1986 and SIA 2018; UK Space Agency holds policy.
Europe: ESA is not a regulator; CNES, BMWK/DLR, and the Luxembourg Space Agency license at national level.
Japan: Cabinet Office NSPS under the 2016 Space Activities Act; JAXA is the operator, not the regulator.
Australia: ASA under the 2018 Space (Launches and Returns) Act and 2019 Rules.
International: ITU-R Radio Regulations for spectrum and orbital coordination via the Master International Frequency Register.

sections.heading: Major frameworks and standards
paragraphs: Space compliance rests on a stack of statutes, regulations, and technical standards. The statutes establish jurisdiction. The regulations implement licensing. The standards define what acceptable engineering looks like. A working operator must navigate all three layers simultaneously, and a single piece of hardware typically must satisfy two or three overlapping standards regimes because primes, customers, and insurers tailor differently.

FAA 14 CFR Part 450 is the consolidated US launch and reentry licensing regulation, issued in December 2020. It replaced the legacy parts 415, 417, 431, and 435 with a single performance-based regime structured across six subparts covering general provisions, application content, safety requirements, terms and conditions, additional requirements, and compliance monitoring. The transition deadline for legacy licences to convert to Part 450 was March 10, 2026. Part 450 mandates a quantitative safety analysis demonstrating expected casualty below 1×10^-4 collective and individual risk thresholds, a flight-hazard analysis, and a system safety programme.

FCC 47 CFR Part 25 governs satellite communications. It covers geostationary and non-geostationary space-station authorisations, earth-station licences, blanket-licensed VSAT networks, earth stations in motion, and market-access grants for foreign-licensed systems. Applicants file FCC Form 312 with a Schedule S technical exhibit and an orbital-debris mitigation showing. The Commission's September 2022 order, FCC 22-74, shortened the post-mission disposal deadline for low-Earth-orbit satellites from twenty-five years to five years, effective September 29, 2024.

NOAA's 15 CFR Part 960 implements the Commercial Remote Sensing regime under the Land Remote Sensing Policy Act, codified at 51 U.S.C. chapter 601. It establishes a three-tier classification based on whether the unenhanced data produced is substantially similar to data already available from foreign or US-licensed sources; Tier 3 systems with unique capability face the strictest conditions including potential temporary operating restrictions in support of national security. India's Indian Space Policy 2023 and the Norms, Guidelines and Procedures 2024 published by IN-SPACe define the operational rulebook for non-governmental entities; only Indian entities may apply directly, and foreign founders must route through a subsidiary or joint venture.

On the technical side, the European Cooperation for Space Standardization publishes the ECSS series, organised into Management, Engineering, Quality, and Space Sustainability branches; ECSS-E-ST-10C governs system engineering, ECSS-Q-ST-70C governs materials and processes, and ECSS-U-AS-10C Rev. 2 implements debris mitigation aligned with ISO 24113. NASA Goddard's GSFC-STD-7000 Rev. B, known as the General Environmental Verification Standard or GEVS, defines baseline environmental verification methods for payloads and is the de facto smallsat test baseline. MIL-STD-1540 originated at The Aerospace Corporation in 1974 and defines vibration, acoustic, shock, and thermal test categories at unit, subsystem, and vehicle level. MIL-STD-461G defines electromagnetic emissions and susceptibility test families and is adopted by NASA as the referenced EMI/EMC standard for US space hardware.
bullets: FAA 14 CFR Part 450: consolidated launch and reentry licensing, performance-based, six subparts, legacy conversion deadline March 10, 2026.
FCC 47 CFR Part 25: satellite communications, Form 312 plus Schedule S, five-year deorbit rule under FCC 22-74.
NOAA 15 CFR Part 960: commercial remote sensing, three-tier classification, statutory authority at 51 U.S.C. ch. 601.
IN-SPACe Indian Space Policy 2023 and NGP 2024: six activity categories, Indian-entity requirement for applicants.
ECSS series: ECSS-E (engineering), ECSS-Q (product assurance), ECSS-M (management), ECSS-U (sustainability), aligned with ISO 24113.
NASA GSFC-STD-7000 Rev. B: General Environmental Verification Standard, baseline for vibration, shock, thermal vacuum, EMI/EMC.
MIL-STD-1540: vibration, acoustic, shock, and thermal qualification categories for launch and space vehicles.
MIL-STD-461G: EMI/EMC interface standard with CE, CS, RE, and RS test families.
ITU Radio Regulations: Advance Publication Information, Coordination Request, and Notification stages with seven-year bringing-into-use deadlines.

sections.heading: The compliance lifecycle for a space company
paragraphs: Compliance does not start at launch and it does not end at separation. It runs continuously from initial concept through end-of-life disposal, and each phase has its own set of regulators, deliverables, and gating reviews. Treating compliance as a downstream activity is the single most common scheduling error in commercial space programmes: by the time hardware is ready to test, the licensing path is often six to twelve months behind.

Concept phase work is dominated by regulatory triage. The operator must determine which jurisdictions have authority (launching state, state of registry, state of effective control), classify the spacecraft and payload under ITAR USML Category XV or EAR 9x515 ECCNs, identify the licensing regulators that will require parallel filings, and scope the spectrum filings the mission will need. ITU Advance Publication Information should typically be filed within months of concept lock for any new frequency assignment because coordination from API to Master International Frequency Register entry routinely takes two to four years.

Design phase deliverables shift toward analytical rather than documentary. The orbital-debris mitigation plan must be drafted to FCC standards and, in parallel, to ISO 24113 if European customers are in scope. The collision-avoidance concept of operations, the disposal plan demonstrating post-mission disposal within five years for FCC-licensed LEO satellites, and the radiation environment characterisation must all be defensible at preliminary design review. For launch-vehicle programmes, the Part 450 pre-application consultation with FAA AST should be initiated; industry practice now treats this as effectively mandatory and runs it for six to eighteen months before formal application.

Qualification phase work executes the environmental verification campaign described in the next section. Each test is documented with a test procedure approved in advance, a test report reviewed against the predicted environments, and non-conformance reports for any deviations. The qualification campaign typically gates the formal licence application: regulators expect to see qualification evidence with the safety case. Launch phase compliance is operational: launch-day flight rules, range coordination, NOTAM and NOTMAR issuance, collision-on-launch screening with US Space Command, and post-launch reporting to FAA AST and FCC. Operational phase compliance is ongoing for the spacecraft lifetime: orbital state vector reporting, manoeuvre notifications, anomaly reports, and licence renewal filings.

End-of-life triggers the disposal compliance regime. For LEO satellites under FCC jurisdiction, post-mission disposal within five years is now mandatory. For larger spacecraft, controlled deorbit with casualty risk demonstrated below the standard 1×10^-4 threshold is required. Final reports to the registering state under the 1976 Registration Convention close the international compliance loop.
bullets: Concept: jurisdiction triage, export-control classification, ITU API filing.
Design: debris mitigation plan, disposal plan, FAA Part 450 pre-application consultation, FCC Schedule S drafting.
Qualification: environmental test campaign documented against tailored standards, non-conformance management.
Launch: range coordination, NOTAMs, conjunction screening, real-time flight rules.
Operations: state vector reporting, manoeuvre notifications, anomaly and mishap reports, licence renewals.
End-of-life: post-mission disposal within five years for FCC LEO, controlled deorbit casualty analysis, registry closure.

sections.heading: Environmental qualification: what spacecraft must survive
paragraphs: Environmental qualification is the test campaign that demonstrates a spacecraft will survive launch and operate in vacuum, with quantified margins above predicted in-flight conditions. The campaign is structured around a tailored requirements specification, typically built from ECSS-E-ST-10-03C, NASA GSFC-STD-7000 Rev. B (GEVS), or a customer-specific document derived from MIL-STD-1540. Each environment is tested at unit, subsystem, and vehicle level, with qualification at higher margins than acceptance and protoqualification used where flight units double as qualification units.

Electromagnetic compatibility testing under MIL-STD-461G or ECSS-E-ST-20-07C verifies that the spacecraft neither emits unacceptable interference (CE conducted emissions, RE radiated emissions) nor degrades when subjected to expected ambient fields (CS conducted susceptibility, RS radiated susceptibility). Practical pain points include intersystem EMC between payload and bus, harness routing impacts on radiated emissions, and the difficulty of reproducing the launch vehicle's RF environment in a ground anechoic chamber.

Mechanical environments dominate the launch profile. Random vibration testing on an electrodynamic shaker verifies survival of the broadband acoustic-induced vibration during ascent, typically 14.1 g RMS for a GEVS general-environment qualification. Sine-vibration testing simulates low-frequency transient loads from pogo and gust. Acoustic testing in a reverberant chamber subjects the structure to direct acoustic loading at 138 dB or higher overall sound pressure level. Shock testing reproduces pyrotechnic separation events using resonant fixtures or live pyros; the GEVS qualification shock spectrum reaches several thousand g at high frequencies. Mass properties verification confirms centre of gravity, moments of inertia, and total mass against the launch vehicle interface control document.

Thermal-vacuum testing in a chamber pumped below 1×10^-5 torr cycles the spacecraft across its qualification temperature range, demonstrating survival and operability at hot and cold extremes with adequate margin. A typical campaign runs four to eight cycles between roughly -30 and +70 degrees Celsius for a LEO commercial spacecraft, with longer dwells at hot and cold for bakeout and characterisation. Thermal-balance testing, typically conducted in the same facility, validates the thermal model used to predict on-orbit temperatures. Outgassing testing under ASTM E595 or ECSS-Q-ST-70-02C measures total mass loss and collected volatile condensable material from materials and assemblies; non-compliant outgassing is a leading cause of optical contamination on coatings, lenses, and solar cells.

Documentation overhead is non-trivial. Each environment requires a test procedure approved before test, a test report cross-referenced to the requirements specification, a non-conformance log, and traceable calibration records for all instrumentation. For ESA-funded missions the configuration management overhead from ECSS-M-ST-40 and Q-ST-10 routinely surprises first-time European-prime suppliers. Programmes selling into NASA, ESA, JAXA, and DoD primes often must satisfy overlapping ECSS, GEVS, and MIL-STD-1540 requirements; the same hardware is sometimes requalified two or three times for different customers.
bullets: EMC: MIL-STD-461G and ECSS-E-ST-20-07C, conducted and radiated emissions and susceptibility families.
Random vibration: broadband ascent environment, typically 14.1 g RMS qualification under GEVS.
Sine vibration: low-frequency transients from pogo and gust.
Acoustic: reverberant chamber, 138 dB or higher overall sound pressure level.
Shock: pyrotechnic separation, resonant fixture or live-pyro reproduction.
Thermal vacuum: less than 1×10^-5 torr, four to eight cycles across qualification temperature range.
Thermal balance: validates the thermal model against predicted on-orbit temperatures.
Outgassing: ASTM E595 and ECSS-Q-ST-70-02C, total mass loss and CVCM.
Mass properties: centre of gravity, moments of inertia, and mass against the launch vehicle ICD.

sections.heading: Licensing process and realistic timelines
paragraphs: Licensing timelines determine launch dates. The single most important schedule lever in any commercial space programme is the regulatory critical path, and the regulator that gates the programme varies by mission. For a launch operator it is FAA AST. For a commercial communications satellite it is the FCC and the host national administration's ITU filings. For an Indian start-up it is IN-SPACe. The timelines below are drawn from the regulators' published guidance and from public licensing records.

For an FAA Part 450 launch licence the formal statutory clock is 180 days from a complete application, but the pre-application consultation period that precedes formal filing routinely runs six to eighteen months. Industry analysis from late 2025 documents that only a handful of operators had completed Part 450 conversions, and SpaceX's Bill Gerstenmaier publicly warned that the licensing system was at risk of collapse ahead of the March 10, 2026 legacy sunset. The performance-based nature of Part 450 requires the applicant to propose a means of compliance for each requirement; FAA has been slow to publish acceptable means of compliance guidance, leaving applicants negotiating bespoke approaches.

For FCC satellite authorisations the timeline depends on whether the application proceeds under streamlined small-satellite processing (47 CFR 25.122 or 25.123) or full Part 25 processing. Streamlined applications target 90 days; full applications routinely run twelve to twenty-four months, particularly where coordination with incumbent operators is required. A Schedule S exhibit captures the technical detail; the orbital-debris mitigation plan is a separate showing.

For IN-SPACe authorisation the published procedure is a two-step path: an initial in-principle authorisation establishing eligibility and a final authorisation issued before operations commence. Timelines are still maturing; published cases indicate three to nine months for the in-principle stage and a further three to six months for the final authorisation, depending on activity category. The NGP 2024 broadens authorisation scope to include in-orbit transfer of ownership and post-mission disposal plans, and applicants are negotiating bespoke conditions because numerical safety thresholds have not all been published.

ITU coordination operates on its own clock. Advance Publication Information is published in the BR International Frequency Information Circular within months of filing. Coordination requests under Article 9 of the Radio Regulations typically run two to four years from filing to entry in the Master International Frequency Register. Bringing-into-use deadlines are seven years from filing for geostationary satellites; WRC-19 Resolution 35 imposes milestone-based deadlines on non-geostationary constellations of 10 per cent deployed within two years, 50 per cent within five, and 100 per cent within seven.
bullets: FAA Part 450: 180-day statutory review, 6 to 18 months pre-application consultation in practice.
FCC Part 25 streamlined small-sat: 90-day target; full Part 25: 12 to 24 months.
NOAA Part 960: 60 to 120 days for Tier 1, longer for Tier 2 and 3 with national-security review.
IN-SPACe: 3 to 9 months in-principle, plus 3 to 6 months final authorisation.
UK SIA 2018: 12 to 24 months for first orbital operator licence, application fee £6,500 under OSA 1986.
ITU API to MIFR: typically 2 to 4 years; bringing-into-use deadline 7 years for GSO.

sections.heading: Pain points: why compliance slips launches
paragraphs: Schedule slips in commercial space programmes are now more often driven by regulatory and qualification work than by hardware. The pattern is consistent across operators and jurisdictions, and the failure modes are predictable enough that they should be treated as design constraints rather than risks.

Pre-application consultation drag is the single largest schedule killer under FAA Part 450. Applicants commonly spend months in iterative correspondence with AST before they can formally file. The performance-based regime requires the applicant to propose a means of compliance for each safety requirement, but FAA has not published acceptable means of compliance for many subparts, so each applicant negotiates bespoke approaches. The result is a backlog in which only a small number of operators completed Part 450 conversions ahead of the March 10, 2026 legacy sunset.

Multi-agency coordination overhead is the second structural pain point. A single launch of a LEO communications satellite from a US range can require simultaneous approvals or coordination from FAA AST, FCC, NOAA if hosting earth-observation payloads, the Department of State for ITAR, the Department of Commerce for EAR, the Department of Defense for federal range use, NASA for ISS proximity operations, and the host range itself. Misaligned safety and coordination requirements between FAA, DoD, and FCC at federal ranges are a documented source of slips.

Export control mis-classification is high-consequence. Spacecraft and many components remain on the USML Category XV under ITAR; ground systems, communications payloads, and many satellites have shifted to EAR 9x515 ECCNs after the 2014 export-control reform and the 2024 expansion that added the License Exception CSA for commercial space activities. A single mis-classification carries strict-liability exposure: civil penalties up to roughly $1 million per violation under ITAR, criminal exposure, and government-contracting debarment. Even an internal email containing controlled technical data sent to a non-US person constitutes a deemed export and triggers liability.

The FCC five-year deorbit rule has reshaped small-satellite design. LEO operators must now demonstrate disposal within five years of mission end, a major design driver for propulsion, drag augmentation, and on-board autonomy for operators that previously assumed natural decay would satisfy the legacy 25-year guideline. Non-US operators serving the US market are also captured.

Cross-cutting standards burden is the slowest-moving pain point. Programmes selling into ESA, NASA, JAXA, or DoD primes typically must comply with overlapping standards regimes including ECSS, GEVS, and MIL-STD-1540 or 461. Each prime tailors differently, and configuration management overhead under ECSS-M-ST-40 and Q-ST-10 routinely surprises first-time European-prime suppliers. For India, the NGP 2024 imposes an Indian-entity requirement: foreign founders and investors must establish an Indian subsidiary or joint venture before applying. For the United Kingdom, third-party liability insurance under the Space Industry Act 2018 remains a perennial choke point because few underwriters are willing to write the required cover at the scales mandated.
bullets: Pre-application drag at FAA AST: 6 to 18 months before formal Part 450 filing.
Multi-agency coordination: typically 3 to 6 simultaneous regulators per launch.
ITAR vs EAR misclassification: civil penalties up to ~$1M per violation, strict liability, deemed-export risk.
Five-year deorbit rule: drives propulsion, drag augmentation, and autonomy onto small-sat platforms.
ECSS, GEVS, and MIL-STD-1540 overlap: same hardware requalified 2 to 3 times for different primes.
IN-SPACe Indian-entity requirement: foreign applicants must form an Indian subsidiary or JV.
UK SIA 2018: third-party liability insurance availability is a structural constraint.

sections.heading: How autonomous AI agents are changing compliance work
paragraphs: The compliance workload described above is dominated by document production, cross-reference, and traceability rather than novel analysis. A Part 450 application runs to several thousand pages of safety case, hazard analysis, and procedure documentation that must be internally consistent and traceable to source requirements. A satellite Schedule S, an orbital-debris mitigation showing, and an ITU coordination filing share substantial overlapping technical content but in different formats. ESA configuration management requirements under ECSS-M-ST-40 generate further documentary overhead.

This is the work where autonomous AI agents are now beginning to be applied. Used carefully, agents can maintain requirement-to-evidence traceability across thousands of documents, reformat the same technical content for different regulators, draft non-conformance reports against a controlled vocabulary, and surface inconsistencies between the qualification test report and the safety analysis. They are not yet substitutes for the engineer who decides what the requirement means or for the licensing professional who negotiates with the regulator.

Invariant's position is that this work is currently bottlenecked by document production rather than by judgement, and that agentic workflows can reduce the production burden without altering the underlying regulatory regime. The right unit of agent autonomy is the document, not the decision. Useful agent tasks include cross-referencing a safety analysis against the requirements specification, generating a Schedule S from a frequency plan and link budget, drafting the orbital-debris mitigation narrative from a disposal trajectory, and producing the ECSS-compliant test report from instrumented test data. Each of these tasks has a verifiable output that a human reviewer can check against ground truth.

What agents should not do is exercise unsupervised regulatory judgement. The acceptable means of compliance for a Part 450 requirement, the safety classification of a spacecraft component, the ITAR-versus-EAR classification of a subsystem, and the negotiated terms of an IN-SPACe authorisation are decisions with strict-liability consequences and remain human responsibilities. The realistic near-term gain is a substantial reduction in the document-production timeline that today gates licence filings, qualification reviews, and customer audits, not a change in the underlying regulatory burden.
bullets: Tractable agent work: requirement-to-evidence traceability across large document corpora.
Tractable agent work: reformatting consistent technical content for FAA, FCC, NOAA, ITU, and IN-SPACe.
Tractable agent work: drafting Schedule S exhibits and orbital-debris mitigation narratives from source data.
Tractable agent work: generating ECSS-compliant test reports from instrumented qualification data.
Not tractable: unsupervised export-control classification, regulatory-judgement decisions, negotiated licence terms.
Unit of autonomy: the document, not the decision.

faqs.question: What is space compliance?
faqs.answer: Space compliance is the integrated programme of regulatory licensing, environmental qualification, frequency coordination, export control, and operational reporting that a commercial space operator executes from concept through end-of-life disposal. It spans national licensing (FAA, FCC, NOAA, IN-SPACe, the UK CAA, and others), technical qualification against standards such as ECSS, GEVS, MIL-STD-1540, and MIL-STD-461, and international coordination under the ITU Radio Regulations and the 1967 Outer Space Treaty regime.

faqs.question: Who regulates commercial space activities in the United States?
faqs.answer: Three agencies hold primary jurisdiction. The FAA's Office of Commercial Space Transportation licenses launch, reentry, and spaceports under 14 CFR Part 450. The FCC Space Bureau licenses satellite radio communications and earth stations under 47 CFR Part 25 and enforces orbital-debris rules. NOAA's Office of Space Commerce licenses private remote-sensing satellites under 15 CFR Part 960. Export control sits with the State Department under ITAR and the Department of Commerce BIS under the EAR.

faqs.question: What is FAA Part 450?
faqs.answer: 14 CFR Part 450 is the FAA's consolidated launch and reentry licensing regulation, issued in December 2020, that replaced the legacy parts 415, 417, 431, and 435. It is a performance-based regime structured across six subparts and requires applicants to propose a means of compliance for each safety requirement. The transition deadline for legacy licences to convert to Part 450 was March 10, 2026.

faqs.question: What is the FCC five-year deorbit rule?
faqs.answer: The FCC's September 2022 order FCC 22-74 requires that satellites operating in low Earth orbit under FCC licence or market access be deorbited within five years of mission end, replacing the legacy 25-year guideline. The rule took effect on September 29, 2024 and applies to non-US operators serving the US market. It has become a major design driver for small-satellite propulsion, drag augmentation, and on-board autonomy.

faqs.question: Who licenses satellites in India?
faqs.answer: IN-SPACe, the Indian National Space Promotion and Authorisation Centre, is the single-window authoriser for all non-governmental space activity in India under the Indian Space Policy 2023 and the Norms, Guidelines and Procedures 2024. ISRO is the national space agency and NSIL is the commercial arm. The Directorate General of Civil Aviation's role is limited to airspace clearance and it is not the primary space-licensing authority.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ITAR and EAR for space companies?
faqs.answer: ITAR, the International Traffic in Arms Regulations, governs defence articles on the United States Munitions List including USML Category XV for spacecraft and related items. EAR, the Export Administration Regulations, governs dual-use items including the 9x515 ECCNs for space-related commodities. The 2014 export-control reform moved many commercial spacecraft items from ITAR to EAR. A 2024 rule added License Exception CSA for commercial space activities. Misclassification is strict-liability exposure with civil penalties up to roughly $1 million per violation.

faqs.question: What is the Outer Space Treaty of 1967?
faqs.answer: The Treaty on Principles Governing the Activities of States in the Exploration and Use of Outer Space, opened for signature in 1967, is the foundational instrument of international space law. It establishes that outer space is not subject to national appropriation, that states bear international responsibility for national space activities including those of non-governmental entities, and that launching states retain jurisdiction over registered objects. Subsequent treaties on liability, registration, and rescue elaborate the regime.

faqs.question: What standards apply to satellite environmental qualification?
faqs.answer: The dominant baselines are NASA Goddard's GSFC-STD-7000 Rev. B, known as GEVS, the European Cooperation for Space Standardization's ECSS-E-ST-10-03C, and the Department of Defense's MIL-STD-1540 series. EMI/EMC testing typically follows MIL-STD-461G or ECSS-E-ST-20-07C. Each customer or prime tailors the specification, so the same hardware may be requalified for different programmes.

faqs.question: How long does it take to get an FAA Part 450 launch licence?
faqs.answer: The statutory review period is 180 days from a complete application. In practice the pre-application consultation period that precedes formal filing routinely runs 6 to 18 months because the performance-based regime requires the applicant to negotiate acceptable means of compliance for each safety requirement. Industry analysis in late 2025 documented a substantial backlog and a small number of completed Part 450 conversions.

faqs.question: What is the ITU and what role does it play in satellite operations?
faqs.answer: The International Telecommunication Union is a United Nations specialised agency that administers the Radio Regulations treaty governing global radio-frequency and orbital coordination. Operators do not file directly with the ITU; they file through their national administration, which submits Advance Publication Information, a Coordination Request, and a Notification to record the assignment in the Master International Frequency Register. Coordination from API to MIFR typically takes 2 to 4 years.

faqs.question: Do small satellites need a separate licensing process?
faqs.answer: Yes in part. The FCC operates a streamlined small-satellite authorisation process under 47 CFR 25.122 and 25.123 with a 90-day target review, available to systems meeting size, lifetime, manoeuvrability, and orbital-debris criteria. Systems not meeting the streamlined criteria proceed under full Part 25. Small satellites still require ITU coordination, export-control classification, and, if hosting earth-observation payloads, a NOAA Part 960 licence.

faqs.question: What is ECSS and is it mandatory?
faqs.answer: ECSS, the European Cooperation for Space Standardization, publishes the dominant European space standards across Management, Engineering, Quality, and Space Sustainability branches. ECSS is mandatory by contract for ESA-funded missions and widely adopted by European primes; for non-ESA programmes it is incorporated where customers contractually require it. ECSS-U-AS-10C Rev. 2 implements debris mitigation aligned with ISO 24113.

faqs.question: What licences does a commercial satellite operator need?
faqs.answer: A typical US LEO communications satellite requires an FCC Part 25 space-station authorisation, ITU frequency coordination through the FCC, earth-station licences for any US ground stations, an export-control classification under ITAR or EAR, and, if hosting remote-sensing payloads, a NOAA Part 960 licence. International operations may require host-country market-access grants and additional national licences. The launch itself requires an FAA launch licence held by the launch operator.

faqs.question: What is the United Kingdom's space licensing regime?
faqs.answer: The United Kingdom operates a two-track regime. The Outer Space Act 1986 governs UK-person space activities conducted overseas, with an application fee of £6,500 per single orbital application. The Space Industry Act 2018 governs UK-domestic launch and orbital activity, with separate licences for launch operators, spaceports, range control, and orbital operators. Both regimes are administered by the Civil Aviation Authority's Space Regulation team.

faqs.question: How are autonomous AI agents being used in space compliance work?
faqs.answer: Agent applications today are focused on document production rather than regulatory judgement: maintaining requirement-to-evidence traceability across large document corpora, reformatting consistent technical content for different regulators, generating Schedule S exhibits and orbital-debris narratives from source data, and producing ECSS-compliant test reports from instrumented qualification data. Export-control classification and other strict-liability decisions remain human responsibilities.
